## Service-Specific Technologies

### 1. Web Applications & Websites

Build custom web applications, marketing websites, eCommerce platforms, business portals, and backend APIs.

#### Frontend Technologies

| Category | Technologies | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Framework** | React 19+, Next.js 16+ | SSR/SSG for performance; Turbopack default |
| **Alternative** | Vue 3, Nuxt 3 | Client preference |
| **Language** | TypeScript 5.9+ | Strict mode enabled |
| **Styling** | Tailwind CSS 4.x, SCSS | CSS-first config, 5x faster builds |
| **UI Components** | shadcn/ui 3.x, Radix UI | Accessible components |
| **State Management** | Zustand, TanStack Query | Minimal Redux |
| **Forms** | React Hook Form, Zod | Schema validation |
| **Animation** | Framer Motion, GSAP | Performance-optimized |
| **Accessibility** | axe-core, Lighthouse CI, eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y | WCAG 2.2 AA compliance required for all web deliverables |
| **Internationalization** | next-intl (Next.js), react-i18next, FormatJS | Required when project serves multiple locales; ICU MessageFormat for plurals/gender |

#### Backend Technologies

| Category | Technologies | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Runtime** | Node.js 22 LTS | Active LTS; Node.js 24 target after Oct 2026 LTS promotion |
| **Alt Runtime** | Bun | Performance-critical services; Node.js API compatible |
| **Framework** | Express 5.x | REST APIs; Express 5 default on npm since Mar 2025 |
| **Alt Framework** | Fastify 5.x | Use when p95 latency < 50 ms is a hard requirement |
| **Alt Framework** | Hono | Lightweight, edge-first, Web Standards API |
| **Language** | TypeScript 5.9+ | Full type safety |
| **API Design** | REST, GraphQL (Yoga preferred, Apollo for existing), tRPC | OpenAPI documentation; tRPC for TS-only stacks |
| **Authentication** | Access tokens (JWT ES256 via `jose`), refresh tokens (opaque, server-stored), OAuth 2.0 + PKCE | Per STD-SEC-003 session model; no localStorage token storage |
| **Auth Libraries** | `jose` (JWT), `@oslojs/crypto` (hashing/TOTP), Passport.js (legacy) | `jose` + `@oslojs` preferred for new projects; Passport.js for legacy migration only |
| **Validation** | Zod, Joi | Request validation |
| **ORM/Query** | Prisma, Drizzle | Type-safe queries; Prisma default for new projects |
| **Migrations** | Prisma Migrate (default), Drizzle Kit, Alembic (Python), Flyway (JVM) | Every project MUST use managed migrations; raw DDL forbidden in production |
| **Email** | Resend (preferred), SendGrid | Transactional email; Resend for new projects; SendGrid for high-volume or legacy |
| **Background Jobs** | BullMQ (Redis-backed), Cloud Tasks | BullMQ for Node.js job queues; Cloud Tasks for serverless/lightweight |
| **Scheduled Jobs** | Cloud Scheduler (preferred), node-cron | Cloud Scheduler for production; node-cron for local dev only |
| **Connection Pooling** | Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, PgBouncer | Required for Cloud Run / serverless; Prisma Data Proxy as alternative |
| **File Storage** | GCS + signed URLs, Sharp (image processing) | Cloudinary or Imgproxy for CDN-backed image transformation |
| **API Codegen** | openapi-typescript, orval | Generate typed clients from OpenAPI specs; required for cross-service contracts |
| **Logging** | pino (preferred), winston | Structured JSON logging; pino for performance; pairs with OpenTelemetry via pino-opentelemetry-transport |
| **Env Management** | `@t3-oss/env-nextjs` (Next.js), `envalid` (Node.js), `dotenv` | Typed env validation required; `dotenv` for local dev only; production secrets via Secret Manager |
| **Date/Time** | date-fns (preferred), dayjs | date-fns for tree-shaking and immutability; dayjs for drop-in Moment replacement; avoid Moment.js (HOLD) |
| **Rich Text Editor** | TipTap (preferred), Lexical | TipTap (ProseMirror-based) for content-rich apps; Lexical for Meta-ecosystem projects |
| **Webhook Delivery** | Svix, or custom via BullMQ + HMAC signing | Reliable outbound webhooks with retry, idempotency, and payload signing per STD-ENG-003 |

#### eCommerce Technologies

| Category | Technologies | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Payments** | Stripe, PayPal, Square | PCI compliant |
| **Subscriptions** | Stripe Billing | Recurring payments |
| **Platforms** | Shopify API, WooCommerce | Integrations |
| **Inventory** | Custom, Shopify | Real-time sync |

#### CMS Options

| Type | Technologies | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Headless** | Sanity, Strapi, Contentful | Modern JAMstack |
| **Traditional** | WordPress (headless) | Blog-heavy sites; **HOLD** — use for legacy only; migrate to Sanity/Strapi |
| **Simple** | Notion API, Airtable | MVP/internal |

#### API Development

| Category | Technologies | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| **REST** | Express + OpenAPI/Swagger | Auto-documentation |
| **Type-Safe API** | tRPC | End-to-end type safety for TypeScript-only stacks |
| **GraphQL** | Yoga (new projects), Apollo Server (existing) | Yoga preferred — lighter runtime; Apollo for legacy |
| **Real-time** | Socket.IO, WebSockets | Live updates |
| **Rate Limiting** | express-rate-limit, @upstash/ratelimit (serverless) | DDoS protection; sliding-window or token-bucket per STD-SEC-003 |
| **Health Checks** | `GET /healthz` (liveness), `GET /readyz` (readiness) | Required for all deployable services; response must include dependency status |
| **Graceful Shutdown** | Built-in `server.close()` + SIGTERM handler | Drain in-flight requests before exit; mandatory for Cloud Run / K8s |

---

## Security Standards

> Security controls below align with the [Authentication & Identity Standard (STD-SEC-003)](./STD-SEC-003%20CYBERCUBE-Authentication-Identity-Standard-v1.md) and the [Cryptography & Key Management Standard (STD-SEC-005)](./STD-SEC-005%20CYBERCUBE-Cryptography-Key-Management-Standard-v1.md). Where this document summarizes, the referenced standard is authoritative.

### Authentication & Authorization

| Category | Standard | Implementation |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Password Hashing** | **Argon2id** (mandatory for new systems) | OWASP #1; bcrypt (cost 12+) accepted in legacy only with documented migration plan |
| **Access Tokens** | Short-lived JWT (ES256 preferred, HS256 acceptable) | No PII in payload; claims limited to subject ID and scope; transmitted over TLS only |
| **Refresh Tokens** | Long-lived, opaque, server-stored | Rotation on use; bound to device/session; revocable |
| **Session Management** | Server-side sessions with httpOnly / secure / sameSite=strict cookies | Session ID: CSPRNG 32+ bytes; idle timeout 30 min; absolute timeout 24 h; regenerate on privilege change (per STD-SEC-003 §4) |
| **OAuth** | OAuth 2.0 + PKCE | Third-party auth; PKCE mandatory even for confidential clients |
| **MFA** | **TOTP (speakeasy)** or **WebAuthn** | Enforced for admin and privileged roles; speakeasy is approved TOTP library (ADR-13) |
| **MFA — Excluded** | SMS OTP | **HOLD** — not recommended due to SIM-swap and SS7 interception risks; use only as fallback-of-last-resort with risk acceptance by ARB |
| **RBAC** | Role-based access | Principle of least privilege |
| **CSRF** | Double-submit cookie or SameSite strict | Required for mutation endpoints |
| **CSP** | Content Security Policy headers | Required for all web applications |
| **Security Headers** | helmet (Express), secure-headers (Fastify) | Enforces CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options; must be first middleware |
| **CORS** | cors (Express), @fastify/cors | Allowlist-only origin policy; no wildcard `*` in production |

### Data Protection

| Category | Standard | Implementation |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Encryption at Rest** | AES-256 | Database, storage |
| **Encryption in Transit** | TLS 1.3 | All connections |
| **Secrets** | Secret Manager | Never in code |
| **PII** | GDPR/CCPA compliance | Data minimization |

### API Security

| Category | Standard | Implementation |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Rate Limiting** | Per-user/IP limits | DDoS protection |
| **Input Validation** | Zod schemas | All endpoints |
| **CORS** | Strict origin policy | Whitelist domains |
| **Headers** | Helmet.js | Security headers |

### Client-Side Encryption

| Category | Standard | Implementation |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Browser Crypto** | Web Crypto API | Native browser cryptography for client-side operations |
| **Library** | `libsodium.js` (tweetnacl) | Use when Web Crypto API is insufficient (e.g. NaCl box/secretbox) |
| **Scope** | Required for healthcare, finance, or client-mandated E2E encryption | ADR required; coordinate with STD-SEC-005 |

### Feature Flags

| Category | Standard | Implementation |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Platform** | LaunchDarkly (enterprise), GrowthBook (open-source), PostHog | LaunchDarkly for paid clients needing audit trails; GrowthBook default for internal |
| **Mobile** | Firebase Remote Config | Flutter / React Native feature toggles |
| **Governance** | All flags MUST have an owner and expiry date | Stale flags (> 90 days unused) must be removed |

### Dependency Management

| Category | Standard | Implementation |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Scanning** | Snyk, npm audit | Automated |
| **Updates** | Dependabot | Weekly PRs |
| **Lock Files** | pnpm-lock.yaml / package-lock.json | Deterministic; pnpm preferred |

---

## Technology Selection Guidelines

### When to Use What

#### Frontend Framework Selection

| Scenario | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Full-stack web app | **Next.js** | SSR, API routes, file routing |
| SPA with existing API | **Vite + React** | Fast builds, simple setup |
| Content-heavy site | **Next.js + Sanity** | CMS integration |
| Vue preference | **Nuxt 3** | Vue ecosystem |

#### Mobile Framework Selection

| Scenario | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| iOS + Android, budget-conscious | **Flutter** | Single codebase, native feel |
| JavaScript team, quick MVP | **React Native** | Familiar stack |
| Maximum iOS performance | **Swift/SwiftUI** | Native APIs |
| Maximum Android performance | **Kotlin/Compose** | Native APIs |
| Complex graphics/AR | **Native** | GPU access |

#### Backend Framework Selection

| Scenario | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Standard web API | **Node.js + Express 5.x** | Team expertise |
| High-performance API | **Fastify 5.x**, Hono, or Go | Lower latency; Fastify/Hono are TRIAL — ADR required |
| AI/ML integration | **Python + FastAPI** | ML ecosystem |
| Enterprise client | **Java Spring Boot** | Industry standard |

#### Database Selection

| Scenario | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Transactional data | **PostgreSQL 17+ / MySQL 8.0+** | ACID, relations; PostgreSQL default for new projects |
| Flexible schema | **MongoDB** | Document store |
| Caching layer | **Redis** | In-memory speed |
| Full-text search | **Elasticsearch** | Search features |
| AI embeddings | **Pinecone / pgvector** | Vector similarity |

---

## Technology Radar

> The Technology Radar is the **authoritative governance model** for technology adoption at CYBERCUBE. It aligns with the [Architecture Governance Policy (POL-GOV-002) §6](./POL-GOV-002%20CYBERCUBE-Architecture-Governance-Policy-v1.md) and is reviewed **quarterly** by the Architecture Review Board (ARB).

### Radar Rings

| Ring | Meaning | Usage | New Project Rule |
|------|---------|-------|------------------|
| **ADOPT** | Proven, recommended for broad use | Default choice for all projects | Use without ADR |
| **TRIAL** | Worth pursuing, validated in pilots | Allowed on low-risk projects with ADR | ADR required (Principal approval) |
| **ASSESS** | Worth exploring, understanding impact | Research and spike work only | ADR required (ARB approval) |
| **HOLD** | Not recommended for new work; phase out | No new adoption; existing usage must have sunset plan | ADR + ARB for any exception |

### Radar — Languages & Runtimes

| Technology | Ring | Notes |
|------------|------|-------|
| TypeScript 5.9+ | **ADOPT** | Primary language for web frontend and backend |
| JavaScript ES2022+ | **ADOPT** | Legacy support, rapid prototyping |
| Python 3.12+ | **ADOPT** | AI/ML, data processing, automation |
| Dart 3.x | **ADOPT** | Cross-platform mobile (Flutter) |
| Swift 6.2+ | **ADOPT** | Native iOS |
| Kotlin 2.1+ | **ADOPT** | Native Android |
| Go | **TRIAL** | High-performance services, enterprise backend |
| Java (Spring Boot) | **TRIAL** | Enterprise client requirement only |
| .NET (ASP.NET Core) | **TRIAL** | Microsoft-stack client requirement only |
| SQL (PostgreSQL 17+ / MySQL 8.0+) | **ADOPT** | Database queries |

### Radar — Web Frameworks

| Technology | Ring | Notes |
|------------|------|-------|
| React 19+ / Next.js 16+ | **ADOPT** | Primary web frontend |
| Vue 3 / Nuxt 3 | **TRIAL** | Client preference only |
| React Router v7 (Remix) | **ASSESS** | React meta-framework alternative; evaluate for SPA-heavy projects |
| Vite + React | **ADOPT** | SPA with existing API |
| Tailwind CSS 4.x | **ADOPT** | Primary styling |
| shadcn/ui 3.x / Radix UI | **ADOPT** | Accessible component library |
| SCSS | **HOLD** | Legacy projects only; migrate to Tailwind |

|------------|------|-------|

| Hono | **TRIAL** | Edge-first, lightweight |
| Bun | **TRIAL** | Performance-critical services |
| NestJS | **TRIAL** | Enterprise-pattern projects |
| Python + FastAPI + Pydantic | **ADOPT** | AI/ML integration, data-heavy apps; Pydantic mandatory for validation |
| Python + Django | **TRIAL** | Admin-heavy data apps |

| libsodium.js | **TRIAL** | Client-side encryption when Web Crypto is insufficient |
| date-fns | **ADOPT** | Date manipulation; tree-shakable, immutable |
| dayjs | **TRIAL** | Lightweight date library; drop-in Moment.js replacement |
| Moment.js | **HOLD** | Deprecated; migrate to date-fns or dayjs |
| TipTap | **ADOPT** | ProseMirror-based rich text editor; preferred for content-rich apps |
| Lexical | **TRIAL** | Meta's rich text framework; evaluate for React-heavy projects |

| envalid | **ADOPT** | Typed env validation for Node.js |
| pydantic-settings | **ADOPT** | Typed env validation for Python (FastAPI) |
| Svix | **TRIAL** | Managed webhook delivery with retry and signing |
| helmet | **ADOPT** | Express security headers middleware |
| cors (Express) | **ADOPT** | CORS middleware; allowlist-only in production |
| @upstash/ratelimit | **TRIAL** | Serverless rate limiting (Redis-backed) |

### Radar — Security & Auth

| Technology | Ring | Notes |
|------------|------|-------|
| Argon2id | **ADOPT** | Password hashing (mandatory for new systems) |
| bcrypt (cost 12+) | **HOLD** | Legacy only; migrate to Argon2id |
| `jose` (JWT) | **ADOPT** | Token creation/verification |
| `@oslojs/crypto` | **ADOPT** | Hashing, TOTP, crypto primitives |
| Passport.js | **HOLD** | Legacy auth middleware; migrate to `jose` + `@oslojs` for new projects |
| OAuth 2.0 + PKCE | **ADOPT** | Third-party auth |
| TOTP (speakeasy) + WebAuthn | **ADOPT** | MFA |
| SMS OTP | **HOLD** | Not recommended; SIM-swap/interception risk |
| Keycloak | **TRIAL** | Enterprise SSO |
| Supabase Auth / Clerk | **TRIAL** | MVP auth |

## Governance & Compliance

### Compliance Level

This standard is **Mandatory**. All CYBERCUBE projects — internal and client-facing — MUST conform unless a formal deviation is granted.

### Deviation Process

Any deviation from the canonical stack defined in this standard requires a documented exception per the [Policy Exception & Waiver Standard (STD-GOV-003)](./STD-GOV-003%20CYBERCUBE-Policy-Exception-Waiver-Standard-v1.md).

| Deviation Type | Example | Required Artifacts | Approver |
|---------------|---------|-------------------|----------|
| **ADOPT → non-listed technology** | Using a framework not in this standard | ADR + risk assessment | ARB |
| **Using a TRIAL technology in production** | Deploying Hono or Bun to a client project | ADR documenting rationale and rollback plan | Principal Engineer |
| **Using an ASSESS technology** | Evaluating CrewAI in a client engagement | ADR + time-boxed spike proposal | ARB |
| **Using a HOLD technology in new code** | Adding bcrypt to a greenfield service | ADR + migration timeline + ARB exception | ARB + CTO |
| **Version below minimum** | Deploying Node.js 20 instead of 22 LTS | ADR with upgrade timeline (max 90 days) | Domain Lead + Principal |

### ADR Requirement

Per the [Architecture Governance Policy (POL-GOV-002) §2](./POL-GOV-002%20CYBERCUBE-Architecture-Governance-Policy-v1.md):
- **Technology Adoption** decisions (new language, framework, database) require an ADR approved by the ARB.
- **Movement between Radar rings** requires an ADR with the approval level specified in POL-GOV-002 §6.2.
- ADRs MUST reference this standard by ID (STD-ENG-009) and the specific Radar ring being changed.

### Review Cadence

| Activity | Frequency | Owner | Deliverable |
|----------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| Full standard review | Quarterly | Engineering Lead + ARB | Updated version of this document |
| Technology Radar review | Quarterly | ARB | Ring movement decisions, published Radar |
| Version requirements check | Per major release | Platform Team | Updated version table |
| Security alignment review | Semi-annually | Security Team | Cross-reference with STD-SEC-003, STD-SEC-005 |
| Ad-hoc update | When new services are added | Engineering Lead | Patch version of this document |

### Sunset & Migration Paths

Technologies classified as **HOLD** MUST have:

1. **Sunset timeline** — maximum 12 months from HOLD classification to full removal from active codebases.
2. **Migration plan** — documented path from HOLD technology to its ADOPT replacement, filed as an ADR.
3. **Tracking** — all active usages of HOLD technologies tracked in the Architecture Debt Register.
4. **No new adoption** — HOLD technologies MUST NOT be introduced in any new project or module. Violations are Tier 1 deviations requiring ARB + CTO approval.

Current HOLD items and their replacement paths:

| HOLD Technology | Replacement | Migration Deadline |
|----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| SCSS | Tailwind CSS 4.x | 2026-10-15 |
| bcrypt | Argon2id | 2026-10-15 |
| SMS OTP | TOTP (speakeasy) / WebAuthn | 2026-10-15 |
| WordPress (headless) | Sanity / Strapi | 2026-10-15 |
| Passport.js | `jose` + `@oslojs/crypto` | 2027-01-15 |
| Apollo Server | GraphQL Yoga | 2027-01-15 |
| Jest | Vitest | 2027-01-15 |

*This document is the authoritative reference for technology decisions at CYBERCUBE. Deviations require formal approval per the process defined in [Governance & Compliance](#governance--compliance).*


# CYBERCUBE Naming & Identifier Standard (v2.0 — umbrella)

**Standard ID:** STD-ENG-001  
**Status:** Active  
**Effective:** 2026-02-10 (v1.2), 2026-04-22 (v1.3 drift-backfill), 2026-04-22 (v2.0 structural split)  
**Classification:** INTERNAL  
**Owner:** Engineering Lead  
**Review Cycle:** Annual + on major version change

> **Umbrella.** This document carries the shared Applicability Tier Table, Glossary, namespace-at-a-glance matrix, and defers-to map for the CYBERCUBE naming and identifier family. Normative content has been **carved into three focused sub-standards** per RFC-0001 (executed 2026-04-22):
>
> - **[49] STD-ENG-001A — Public Entity IDs (CC-PID)** — Namespace B, §2 + §5 + §6 + Support SOP.
> - **[50] STD-ENG-001B — Artifact & Governance Identifiers** — Namespaces A + G, §1 + §3 + §7 (Ticket vs WOE).
> - **[51] STD-ENG-001C — Module, Component & File Naming** — Namespace M, §4 + Developer Cheat Sheet.
>
> All prior v1.x IDs, conventions, and rules remain in force. The sub-standards reproduce the v1.3 content byte-preserved. See the [Namespace-at-a-glance matrix](#namespace-at-a-glance-matrix) and [defers-to map](#defers-to-map) below to find the clause you need.

## Applicability Tier Table

| Applicability | Tier | Summary of Clauses in This Standard | Waiver Path |
| ------------- | ---- | ----------------------------------- | ----------- |
| All projects producing artifacts, IDs, or modules | **T1 MUST** | (1) All standards, policies, and governance artifacts MUST follow the Namespace-A and Namespace-G naming conventions defined here (filenames, IDs, and identifiers). (2) Public entity identifiers exposed to external systems (APIs, URLs, logs) MUST follow the CC-PID format; raw database primary keys MUST NOT be exposed. (3) CC-PIDs MUST NOT be used for authentication or authorization — per STD-SEC-003/004 T1. (4) All new modules/components MUST follow Namespace-M conventions (directory, file, export naming). (5) Entity-code registration MUST precede first use in code; unregistered entity codes MUST NOT appear in production. | None (non-waivable — naming is a coordination standard) |
| SaaS / customer-facing | **T2 SHOULD** | Automated lint for component-type vocabulary (file/class suffixes), directory-structure CI check, module-boundary import lint, CC-PID format validator in API middleware, developer quick-reference cards, onboarding training on namespaces. | Lightweight waiver per POL-GOV-001 §8.3 |
| Regulated / high-risk | **T3 MAY** | CC-PID integrity signature (tamper-evidence), per-tenant namespace partitioning in IDs, identifier audit trail (who created which CC-PID, when), identifier-change workflow with approval, code-generation tools emitting conformant IDs only, pre-commit hooks blocking non-conformant identifiers. | Formal waiver per STD-GOV-003 |

> Per POL-GOV-001 §8.8.

> **v1.3 (2026-04-22) — Applicability Tier Table + status vocabulary normalization.** T1 = five rules enforceable today. Pre-existing non-canonical statuses (`PENDING`, `PARTIAL         `) remapped to `ROADMAP` and `PARTIAL` respectively.

**Quick links:** [Glossary](#glossary) · [0. Purpose](#0-purpose--design-principles) · [Namespace matrix](#namespace-at-a-glance-matrix) · [Defers-to map](#defers-to-map) · [8. Version History](#8-version-history) · [Implementation Status](#implementation-status)
**Sub-standard jumps:** [1. Artifact Naming → [50] STD-ENG-001B §1](<[50]-STD-ENG-001B%20CYBERCUBE-Artifact-Governance-Identifiers-Standard-v1.md>) · [2. Public Entity IDs → [49] STD-ENG-001A §2](<[49]-STD-ENG-001A%20CYBERCUBE-Public-Entity-IDs-Standard-v1.md>) · [3. Governance Registry IDs → [50] §3](<[50]-STD-ENG-001B%20CYBERCUBE-Artifact-Governance-Identifiers-Standard-v1.md>) · [4. Module, Component & File Naming → [51] STD-ENG-001C §4](<[51]-STD-ENG-001C%20CYBERCUBE-Module-Component-File-Naming-Standard-v1.md>) · [5. CC-PID Implementation → [49] §5](<[49]-STD-ENG-001A%20CYBERCUBE-Public-Entity-IDs-Standard-v1.md>) · [6. Tables → [49] §6](<[49]-STD-ENG-001A%20CYBERCUBE-Public-Entity-IDs-Standard-v1.md>) · [7. Tickets vs WOE → [50] §7](<[50]-STD-ENG-001B%20CYBERCUBE-Artifact-Governance-Identifiers-Standard-v1.md>) · [Cheat Sheet → [51]](<[51]-STD-ENG-001C%20CYBERCUBE-Module-Component-File-Naming-Standard-v1.md>) · [Appendix A Support SOP → [49]](<[49]-STD-ENG-001A%20CYBERCUBE-Public-Entity-IDs-Standard-v1.md>)

## Glossary

This glossary defines key terms used throughout the CYBERCUBE Naming & Identifier Standard. All definitions are **normative** unless stated otherwise.

### A

#### ACC (Account)

A top-level organizational entity representing a customer account, tenant, or organization within
CYBERCUBE systems.

An ACC may own users, projects, billing records, and configurations.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Entity Code** | ACC |
| **Notes** | Canonical replacement for "Organization". Used for multi-tenant scoping. Never reused once assigned. |

#### Artifact

Any non-database object produced or managed during software development or operations, including:
- Source files
- Repositories
- Branches
- Build outputs
- Deployment artifacts
- Documentation files

Artifacts use **Namespace A** (Artifact Naming) and may include mutable metadata (version, date, environment).

#### Authentication (AuthN)

The process of verifying the identity of a user or system actor (e.g., via password, OAuth, SSO, magic link).

⚠️ **Critical:** Public Entity IDs MUST NOT be used for authentication.

#### Authorization (AuthZ)

The process of determining what actions an authenticated user or system is allowed to perform.

⚠️ **Critical:** Public Entity IDs MUST NOT be used for authorization or permission checks.

### C

#### Check Digit (CHK)

A single-character suffix derived deterministically from an entity code and token, appended to all Public Entity IDs.

**Purpose:**
- ✅ Detect transcription and manual entry errors
- ❌ Not cryptographic
- ❌ Not a security feature
- ✅ Mandatory in CC-PID v1

**Example:** `INV-4Q7T9P-N`

#### Client (CLT)

A customer entity representing a business or individual receiving services.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Entity Code** | CLT |
| **Relationship** | Typically associated with one ACC |
| **Data Access** | May own projects, invoices, and contacts |

---

### E

#### Entity

A persistent domain object stored in a database and represented by an internal identifier (e.g., UUID) and a Public Entity ID.

**Examples:**
- Account
- User
- Project
- Invoice

Entities use **Namespace B** (Public Entity IDs).

#### Entity Code

A fixed, three-letter uppercase code identifying the type of a Public Entity.

**Examples:** `ACC`, `USR`, `PRJ`, `INV`

**Properties:**
- ✅ Immutable
- ✅ Never reused
- ✅ Used only for classification and routing

#### Environment (Artifact Context)

A deployment or execution context such as:
- **DEV** (Development)
- **STG** (Staging)
- **PRD** (Production)

Used only in artifact naming suffixes.

> ⚠️ **Note:** This is distinct from the ENV Public Entity code.

#### Environment (Entity)

A database entity representing a logical or physical environment configuration.

⚠  Note:

The Entity Code "ENV" is used for system configuration records only and it’s unrelated to
artifact environment suffixes

---

### G

#### Governance Rules

Mandatory policies that define how naming, identifiers, and codes are created, managed, and preserved over time.

**Key principles:**
- ✅ Immutability
- ✅ No reuse
- ✅ Central registry
- ✅ Server-side generation

---

### I

#### Identifier (ID)

A value used to uniquely reference an object.

**In CYBERCUBE, two main types exist:**

| Type | Scope | Visibility |
|------|-------|------------|
| **Internal ID** (e.g., UUIDv7) | System-only | Hidden |
| **Public Entity ID** | User-facing | Exposed |

#### Immutability

The principle that once assigned, an identifier or code must never change.

**Applies to:**
- ✅ Public Entity IDs
- ✅ Entity Codes
- ✅ Historical references

---

### N

#### Namespace

A logical partition defining naming rules, formats, and constraints.

**CYBERCUBE defines two namespaces:**

| Namespace | Purpose | Mutability |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| **Namespace A** | Artifact Naming | Mutable |
| **Namespace B** | Public Entity IDs (CC-PID) | Immutable |

⚠️ **Critical:** Namespaces must never be mixed.

---

### P

#### Permission (PRM)

A granular access rule defining an allowed action within the system.

> ⚠️ **Critical:** The Entity Code "PRM" is used in authorization systems and must NEVER be inferred from Public Entity IDs.

#### Policy (POL)

A higher-level rule or configuration governing system behavior or access control.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Entity Code** | POL |
| **Examples** | Security policies, access policies |

#### Public Entity ID (CC-PID)

A human-readable, non-sensitive identifier used to reference entities externally.

**Canonical format:** `<ENTITY>-<TOKEN>-<CHK>`

**Example:** `PRJ-X2M8KD-F`

**Properties:**
- ✅ Safe to expose
- ✅ Immutable
- ❌ Not a secret
- ❌ Not proof of identity or access

---

### R

#### Reusable Standard Module (RSM)

A top-level project classification for versioned, governed, standalone modules designed to be reused across multiple future projects. RSMs are productized internal assets that sit between one-off projects and product/platform deliverables.

**Classification:**
- ❌ Not a one-off project
- ❌ Not a product or platform
- ✅ A certified building block

**Governance:** Versioned, standalone, subject to certification and reuse governance.

---

### M

#### Module (MOD)

A reusable software unit that exists within products, may be shared across products, and has an independent lifecycle requiring governance, ownership, and dependency tracking.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Registry ID** | MOD-XXX (Namespace G, sequential, immutable) |
| **Scope** | Platform, Core, Domain, Integration, UI, Governance, Experimental |
| **Reusability** | Global, Portfolio, Product |

#### Module Slug

A kebab-case, human-readable name assigned alongside a Module ID. Used in directory paths, import paths, and configuration references.

**Properties:**
- ✅ Derived from the module name (e.g., `identity`, `billing-engine`)
- ✅ Immutable once assigned
- ✅ Registered in the Module Registry alongside MOD-XXX
- ❌ Must NOT contain version, date, or environment metadata

#### Component Type Suffix

A controlled vocabulary term appended to file names to classify the role of a component within a module. Uses dot-notation before the file extension.

**Examples:** `.service`, `.controller`, `.repository`, `.adapter`, `.page`, `.form`, `.chart`

**Properties:**
- ✅ From a controlled vocabulary (architectural or UI)
- ✅ Centrally governed; new suffixes require review
- ❌ Must NOT be invented ad-hoc

---

### N-G

#### Namespace G (Governance Registry Identifiers)

A logical partition for immutable, sequential governance identifiers used to register strategic and architectural assets.

**Format:** `{PREFIX}-{NNN}` (e.g., `PRD-001`, `MOD-003`)

**Properties:**
- ✅ Sequential assignment, registry-controlled
- ✅ Immutable, never reused
- ❌ Not random (unlike CC-PID tokens)
- ❌ Not mutable (unlike Namespace A artifacts)

#### Namespace M (Module, Component & File Naming)

A logical partition defining naming conventions for the internal anatomy of software modules: module identification, component naming, file naming, and directory structure.

---

### T

#### Token

The random component of a Public Entity ID.

**Properties:**
- ✅ Generated using a human-safe alphabet
- ✅ No semantic meaning
- ✅ Unique per (entity_code, token) pair

---

### U

#### User (USR)

An individual human or service account that can authenticate into the system.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Entity Code** | USR |
| **Features** | Belongs to an ACC; Subject to roles and permissions |

---

### V

#### Versioning

The practice of evolving standards while preserving backward compatibility.

**Current standard:** CC-PID v1

**Future changes:** Require a major version increment. All prior versions remain valid indefinitely.

---

## 0. Purpose & Design Principles

This umbrella standard unifies CYBERCUBE's artifact naming, public entity identifiers, governance registry IDs, and module/component/file naming under a single coordination envelope. It does **not** define authentication mechanisms, authorization models, encryption schemes, or API security patterns.

Design principles (unchanged from v1.x):

- Separation of concerns (naming ≠ identifying ≠ authorization)
- Human-safe but system-rigorous identifiers
- Immutability for external references
- Auditability & future-proofing

Four namespaces, carved into three sub-standards (see matrix below), MUST NEVER be mixed.

---

## Namespace-at-a-glance matrix

| Namespace | Owning sub-standard | Audience | Mutability | Example |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|------------|---------|
| **A** — Artifact Naming | [50] STD-ENG-001B | Authors of files, repos, branches, CI/CD, WOE | Mutable | `WA-SS_MyApp_v1.2.0_20250116_PRD.zip`, `CS-ER-1427` |
| **B** — Public Entity IDs (CC-PID v1) | [49] STD-ENG-001A | API, database, UI, support engineers | Immutable, random | `PRJ-X2M8KD-F`, `CLT-9F4K7Q-F` |
| **G** — Governance Registry IDs | [50] STD-ENG-001B | Standards authors, ARB, governance council | Immutable, sequential | `PRD-001`, `MOD-003` (future: `ADR-NNN`, `STD-NNN`) |
| **M** — Module / Component / File | [51] STD-ENG-001C | Every engineer writing code | Convention-based | `modules/identity/src/services/session.service.ts` |

## Defers-to map

| Question / clause | Defers to |
|-------------------|-----------|
| "How do I name a build artifact, file, repo, branch, or WOE?" | [50] STD-ENG-001B §1 |
| "What is a CC-PID? Format, check digit, regex, test vectors?" | [49] STD-ENG-001A §2 |
| "How do I implement CC-PID generation / validation / SDK?" | [49] STD-ENG-001A §5 |
| "Which entity codes are reserved? What's the PARENT-SUB crosswalk?" | [49] STD-ENG-001A §6 |
| "How do I read a CC-PID over the phone (support SOP)?" | [49] STD-ENG-001A Appendix A |
| "What's the format of a PRD-XXX or MOD-XXX governance ID?" | [50] STD-ENG-001B §3 |
| "How do customer tickets relate to internal WOE?" | [50] STD-ENG-001B §7 |
| "How do I name a module, component, file, or directory?" | [51] STD-ENG-001C §4 |
| "Quick-reference cheat sheet for all four namespaces?" | [51] STD-ENG-001C Developer Cheat Sheet |
| "T1 baseline rules (aggregate, all 5)?" | This umbrella, Tier Table above |

## Binding rules (applicable across all sub-standards)

1. **No namespace mixing** — A/B/G/M each have distinct pattern rules; an identifier MUST NOT simultaneously claim two namespaces.
2. **Immutability** — Entity codes, CC-PIDs, MOD-XXX, PRD-XXX, and module slugs, once assigned, are never renamed or reused.
3. **Central registry precedence** — entity-code, governance-ID, and module-slug registries are authoritative; unregistered identifiers MUST NOT appear in production.
4. **CC-PID is not a capability** — binds to [18] STD-SEC-003 and [19] STD-SEC-004 T1: possession of a CC-PID MUST NEVER grant access.
5. **Server-side generation** — CC-PID tokens, governance sequence numbers, and module IDs MUST be assigned by the authoritative service, never by clients.

---

## 8. Version History

| Version | Date | Changes |
|---------|------|---------|
| v1.0 | 2025-12 | Initial release: artifact naming conventions, CC-PID format, entity code registry. |
| v1.1 | 2026-01 | Added mandatory SHA-256 check digit algorithm, cross-language reference implementations (7 languages), SDK contract with standard error codes, batch generation guidance, global uniqueness model and decision matrix, collision handling requirements, rate limiting guidance, Support SOP. |
| v1.2 | 2026-02 | Added Namespace G (Governance Registry Identifiers: PRD-XXX, MOD-XXX). Added Namespace M (Module, Component & File Naming): absorbed Module ID Standard v1, module identification (MOD-XXX), module slugs, Module Records, component type-suffix convention (architectural + UI), hybrid file naming rule, canonical directory structure, module boundary rules, anti-patterns. Renumbered sections 3–6 to 5–8. Updated glossary, cheat sheet, and related documents. |
| v1.3 | 2026-04-22 | **Drift-backfill.** Reconciles filename-vs-header version drift (filename was v1.2, header had claimed v1.3). Applicability Tier Table added and status vocabulary normalized (legacy `PENDING`/`PARTIAL        ` remapped to `ROADMAP`/`PARTIAL`). No semantic rule changes. |
| v2.0 | 2026-04-22 | **Structural split per RFC-0001.** Umbrella shrunk to Tier Table + Glossary + namespace matrix + defers-to map + aggregated history. Normative content carved to [49] STD-ENG-001A (CC-PID), [50] STD-ENG-001B (Artifact + Governance IDs), [51] STD-ENG-001C (Module/Component/File). All v1.x rules preserved byte-for-byte in the sub-standards. Sub-standards start at v1.0 (2026-04-22). |

---

CYBERCUBE Documentation & RFC Standard (v1)

Glossary

This glossary defines key terms used throughout the CYBERCUBE Documentation & RFC
Standard.

All definitions are normative unless stated otherwise.

A

ADR (Architecture Decision Record)

A document capturing a significant architectural decision.

Components:
- Context (why)
- Decision (what)
- Consequences (impact)
- Status (proposed/accepted/deprecated)

Audience

The intended readers of a document.

Categories:
- Engineers (implementers)
- Operators (maintainers)
- Leadership (decision-makers)
- Customers (end users)

Author

The primary creator of a document.

Responsibilities:
- Initial draft
- Incorporating feedback
- Maintaining accuracy
- Tracking status

Approver

A person authorized to approve a document.

Role varies by document type.

C

Champion

The person driving an RFC through the process.

Also known as: Author, Owner

Changelog

A record of changes to a document or system.

See also: Version History

Contributor

Anyone who provides input to a document.

Activities:
- Reviewing
- Commenting
- Suggesting edits

D

Deprecated

A status indicating a document is outdated and should not be used.

Lifecycle: Active → Deprecated → Archived

Document Type

A classification of documentation by purpose.

CYBERCUBE types:
- Standard
- Guide
- RFC
- ADR
- Runbook
- Reference
- Tutorial

Draft

An incomplete document under development.

Not yet approved or published.

E

Effective Date

The date a document becomes active/binding.

Required for: Standards, Policies

F

Feedback Period

A designated time for collecting comments on a document.

RFC minimum: 2 weeks

G

Guide

A document providing recommendations (not requirements).

Compliance: Suggested, not mandatory.

Also known as: Best Practice, How-To

H

Heading

A section title in a document.

Levels: H1 (title), H2 (section), H3 (subsection)

I

Informative

Content that provides context but is not binding.

Contrast with: Normative

L

Lifecycle

The stages a document passes through.

Stages:
- Draft
- Review
- Approved
- Active
- Deprecated
- Archived

M

Maintainer

The person responsible for keeping a document current.

May differ from author after transfer.

Markdown

The markup language for CYBERCUBE documentation.

Format: CommonMark with GFM extensions

Metadata

Structured information about a document.

Fields:
- Title
- Version
- Status
- Author
- Date
- Tags

N

Normative

Content that establishes requirements.

Keywords: MUST, SHALL, REQUIRED

Contrast with: Informative

O

Owner

The person accountable for a document.

Responsibilities:
- Accuracy
- Currency
- Access
- Retirement

P

Policy

A high-level document stating organizational positions.

Enforcement: Mandatory compliance.

Proposal

A document suggesting a change or new capability.

Synonym: RFC

R

Reference

A document providing detailed specifications.

Format: API docs, schemas, configurations

Review

The process of evaluating a document for quality and accuracy.

Types:
- Peer review
- Technical review
- Leadership review

Reviewer

A person who evaluates a document.

Provides feedback, may approve.

RFC (Request for Comments)

A proposal document for significant changes.

Process:
1. Draft proposal
2. Solicit feedback
3. Revise
4. Decide (accept/reject)

Runbook

A document providing step-by-step operational procedures.

Also known as: Playbook, SOP

S

Scope

The boundaries of what a document covers.

States: In scope, out of scope

Section

A major division of a document.

Numbered for standards, titled for guides.

Stakeholder

Anyone affected by a document's content.

Categories:
- Primary (directly affected)
- Secondary (indirectly affected)

Standard

A document establishing mandatory requirements.

Compliance: Required.

Keywords: MUST, SHALL, REQUIRED

Status

The current state of a document.

Values: Draft, Review, Approved, Active, Deprecated, Archived

Style Guide

A document defining writing conventions.

Covers: Tone, formatting, terminology

T

Template

A pre-structured document format.

Purpose: Consistency, completeness

TOC (Table of Contents)

An index of document sections.

Auto-generated for long documents.

V

Version

A unique identifier for a document revision.

Format: `v{major}.{minor}` or `v{major}.{minor}.{patch}`

Version History

A record of document versions and changes.

Location: End of document

---

CYBERCUBE Documentation & RFC Standard (v1)

Standard ID: STD-ENG-007
Status: Active
Effective: 2026-01-17
Applies to: All CYBERCUBE technical documentation

0. Purpose & Design Principles

This standard defines how CYBERCUBE creates, manages, and governs documentation.
It establishes document types, naming conventions, the RFC process for proposing
changes, and ownership models.

Industry alignment:
- IETF RFC Process
- Amazon ADR Model
- Google Design Documents
- Stripe Documentation Standards

Design principles:

1. **Clarity** — Write for the reader, not the writer
2. **Consistency** — Same structure, same conventions
3. **Currency** — Documents reflect current state
4. **Discoverability** — Easy to find, well-organized
5. **Accountability** — Clear ownership
6. **Versioned** — Changes are tracked

This document does NOT define:
- Customer-facing documentation style
- Marketing content
- Legal documents

1. Document Types

CYBERCUBE documentation is classified by type and purpose.

1.1 Document Type Hierarchy

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    DOCUMENT TYPE HIERARCHY                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  GOVERNANCE          DECISION           OPERATIONAL                 │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐          │
│  │ Standard     │    │ RFC          │   │ Guide        │          │
│  │ Policy       │    │ ADR          │   │ Runbook      │          │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘   │ Reference    │          │
│        │                   │            │ Tutorial     │          │
│        ▼                   ▼            └──────────────┘          │
│   MUST comply        Captures              │                       │
│   Requirements       decisions             ▼                       │
│                                       SHOULD follow               │
│                                       Recommendations             │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

1.2 Document Types Defined

| Type | Purpose | Compliance | Audience | Examples |
|------|---------|------------|----------|----------|
| Standard | Establish requirements | Mandatory | Engineers, Ops | Naming Standard |
| Policy | State organizational positions | Mandatory | All | Security Policy |
| RFC | Propose significant changes | N/A (process) | Stakeholders | New auth system |
| ADR | Record architectural decisions | Informative | Engineers | Database choice |
| Guide | Provide recommendations | Suggested | Engineers | Code style |
| Runbook | Document procedures | Required when applicable | Ops | Incident response |
| Reference | Detail specifications | Informative | Engineers | API documentation |
| Tutorial | Teach a skill | Informative | Engineers | Getting started |

1.3 Standards vs Guides

| Aspect | Standard | Guide |
|--------|----------|-------|
| Compliance | MUST follow | SHOULD follow |
| Language | Normative (SHALL, MUST) | Advisory (recommend, consider) |
| Approval | Formal review | Team review |
| Exceptions | Documented + approved | At discretion |
| Updates | Versioned, announced | Continuous |

**Standard example:**
> "All public identifiers MUST use the CC-PID format: `{prefix}_{random}`"

**Guide example:**
> "Consider using descriptive variable names that indicate the data type"

1.4 RFC vs ADR

| Aspect | RFC | ADR |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Purpose | Propose change | Record decision |
| Timing | Before decision | After decision |
| Scope | Significant changes | Architectural choices |
| Process | Review + approval | Document + commit |
| Outcome | Accept/Reject | Record of why |

**RFC:** "Should we migrate from PostgreSQL to CockroachDB?"
**ADR:** "We chose PostgreSQL because..."

1.5 Normative Language

Standards use RFC 2119 keywords:

| Keyword | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| MUST / SHALL / REQUIRED | Absolute requirement |
| MUST NOT / SHALL NOT | Absolute prohibition |
| SHOULD / RECOMMENDED | Strong recommendation |
| SHOULD NOT / NOT RECOMMENDED | Strong discouragement |
| MAY / OPTIONAL | Truly optional |

```markdown
<!-- Standard language -->
All API endpoints MUST return JSON responses.
Authentication tokens SHOULD be rotated every 24 hours.
Clients MAY cache responses for up to 5 minutes.

<!-- Guide language -->
We recommend using descriptive error messages.
Consider implementing retry logic for transient failures.
You might want to add logging for debugging purposes.
```

2. Document Structure

All documents follow consistent structure based on type.

2.1 Standard Document Structure

```markdown
# {Standard Name} Standard (v{version})

## Glossary
{Term definitions, alphabetically ordered}

---

## {Standard Name} Standard (v{version})

**Status:** {Draft|Active|Deprecated}
**Effective:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Applies to:** {Scope}

### Applicability Tier Table (REQUIRED)

Per POL-GOV-001 §8.8, every standard MUST populate this table. Clauses in the body MUST be tagged with their tier (T1 / T2 / T3) and reference the row that authorizes them.

| Applicability | Tier | Summary of Clauses in This Standard | Waiver Path |
| ------------- | ---- | ----------------------------------- | ----------- |
| All projects | **T1 MUST** | {List the baseline clauses applicable to every project, no exception} | None (non-waivable) |
| SaaS / customer-facing | **T2 SHOULD** | {List clauses applicable to customer-facing / multi-tenant projects} | Lightweight waiver per POL-GOV-001 §8.3 |
| Regulated / high-risk | **T3 MAY** | {List clauses applicable only to regulated, safety-critical, or high-assurance projects} | Formal waiver with risk assessment per STD-GOV-003 |

> A standard whose body contains normative clauses but lacks a populated Tier Table MUST NOT advance past `Draft` status.

### 0. Purpose & Design Principles
{Why this standard exists, principles guiding it}

### 1. {First Major Section}
{Numbered sections for requirements}

### 2. {Second Major Section}
...

---

## {Standard Name} — Quick Reference Card
{One-page summary for printing}

---

## Implementation Status
{Tracking adoption}

Per POL-GOV-001 §8.9, every row MUST use one of the canonical status values:
`COMPLETE` | `IN PLACE` | `PARTIAL` | `ROADMAP` | `N/A`.

`ROADMAP` rows MUST state (a) the tier at which they become enforceable, and (b) the re-trigger condition that will promote them to `IN PLACE`.

| Component | Status | Tier | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------|
| {...} | {IN PLACE \| COMPLETE \| PARTIAL \| ROADMAP \| N/A} | {T1\|T2\|T3} | {Re-trigger condition if ROADMAP} |

---

## Version History
| Version | Date | Changes |
|---------|------|---------|
```

2.2 RFC Document Structure

```markdown
# RFC-{number}: {Title}

**Status:** {Draft|Review|Accepted|Rejected|Superseded}
**Author:** {Name}
**Created:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Last Updated:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Reviewers:** {Names}

## Summary
{One paragraph overview}

## Motivation
{Why is this change needed?}

## Current State
{How things work today}

## Proposed Solution
{What we're proposing}

## Alternatives Considered
{Other options and why not chosen}

## Implementation Plan
{How we'll implement this}

## Risks & Mitigations
{What could go wrong}

## Open Questions
{Unresolved issues}

## References
{Links to related documents}

---

## Discussion
{Comments and feedback log}

## Decision
**Date:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Outcome:** {Accepted|Rejected|Deferred}
**Rationale:** {Why this decision}
```

2.3 ADR Document Structure

```markdown
# ADR-{number}: {Title}

**Status:** {Proposed|Accepted|Deprecated|Superseded}
**Date:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Decision Makers:** {Names}
**Supersedes:** {ADR-number, if applicable}
**Superseded by:** {ADR-number, if applicable}

## Context
{What is the situation that requires a decision?}

## Decision
{What is the decision?}

## Consequences
{What are the results of this decision?}

### Positive
- {Benefit 1}
- {Benefit 2}

### Negative
- {Drawback 1}
- {Drawback 2}

### Neutral
- {Observation 1}

## Compliance
{How will we ensure this decision is followed?}

## References
{Links to related documents}
```

2.4 Guide Document Structure

```markdown
# {Guide Name} Guide

**Last Updated:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Maintainer:** {Name/Team}
**Status:** {Active|Draft|Deprecated}

## Overview
{What this guide covers and why}

## Prerequisites
{What readers need to know}

## {Topic 1}
{Content with examples}

## {Topic 2}
{Content with examples}

## Best Practices
{Summary of recommendations}

## Common Mistakes
{What to avoid}

## Further Reading
{Links to related resources}
```

2.5 Runbook Document Structure

```markdown
# Runbook: {Procedure Name}

**Last Updated:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Owner:** {Name/Team}
**Review Cycle:** {Quarterly|Annually}

## Overview
{What this runbook covers}

## When to Use
{Trigger conditions}

## Prerequisites
- {Access required}
- {Tools needed}

## Procedure

### Step 1: {Action}
```
{Command or action}
```
**Expected result:** {What should happen}

### Step 2: {Action}
...

## Verification
{How to confirm success}

## Rollback
{How to undo if needed}

## Troubleshooting
| Symptom | Cause | Resolution |
|---------|-------|------------|

## Escalation
{When and to whom}

## References
{Related runbooks, documentation}
```

2.6 Tutorial Document Structure

```markdown
# Tutorial: {Skill or Topic}

**Last Updated:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Maintainer:** {Name/Team}
**Status:** {Active|Draft|Deprecated}
**Difficulty:** {Beginner|Intermediate|Advanced}
**Time to Complete:** {Estimated duration}

## Overview
{What you will learn and why it matters}

## Prerequisites
- {Required knowledge}
- {Required tools/access}

## Step 1: {Action}
{Explanation}
```
{Command or code}
```
**Expected result:** {What should happen}

## Step 2: {Action}
...

## Summary
{Recap of what was covered}

## Next Steps
{Where to go from here}

## Further Reading
{Links to related guides, references}
```

3. Naming & Organization

Consistent naming and organization enables discoverability.

3.1 Document Naming Convention

Format: `{Type}-{Name}-{Version}.md`

> **Note:** Standards within the CYBERCUBE catalog MAY use a numeric prefix
> (e.g., `5.8 CYBERCUBE-...`) to indicate catalog ordering. This prefix is
> a catalog convention and is separate from the document naming format below.

| Type | Prefix | Example |
|------|--------|---------|
| Standard | `CYBERCUBE-{Name}-Standard` | `CYBERCUBE-Naming-Identifier-Standard-v1.1.md` |
| Policy | `CYBERCUBE-{Name}-Policy` | `CYBERCUBE-Security-Policy-v1.md` |
| RFC | `RFC-{number}-{Name}` | `RFC-0042-New-Auth-System.md` |
| ADR | `ADR-{number}-{Name}` | `ADR-0015-PostgreSQL-Selection.md` |
| Guide | `{Name}-Guide` | `TypeScript-Style-Guide.md` |
| Runbook | `Runbook-{Name}` | `Runbook-Database-Failover.md` |
| Reference | `{Name}-Reference` | `API-v1-Reference.md` |

3.2 Numbering Systems

**RFC numbers:**
```
RFC-0001 through RFC-9999
Sequential assignment
Never reused
```

**ADR numbers:**
```
ADR-0001 through ADR-9999
Sequential assignment
Never reused
```

**Standard versions:**
```
v{major}.{minor}
v1.0, v1.1, v2.0
Major: Breaking changes
Minor: Additions/clarifications
```

3.3 Directory Structure

```
docs/
├── standards/
│   ├── CYBERCUBE-Naming-Identifier-Standard-v1.1.md
│   ├── CYBERCUBE-API-Design-Standard-v1.md
│   └── ...
│
├── policies/
│   ├── CYBERCUBE-Security-Policy-v1.md
│   └── ...
│
├── rfcs/
│   ├── RFC-0001-Initial-Architecture.md
│   ├── RFC-0042-New-Auth-System.md
│   └── index.md  (RFC registry)
│
├── adrs/
│   ├── ADR-0001-Use-TypeScript.md
│   ├── ADR-0015-PostgreSQL-Selection.md
│   └── index.md  (ADR registry)
│
├── guides/
│   ├── TypeScript-Style-Guide.md
│   ├── Code-Review-Guide.md
│   └── ...
│
├── runbooks/
│   ├── Runbook-Database-Failover.md
│   ├── Runbook-Incident-Response.md
│   └── index.md  (Runbook index)
│
├── reference/
│   ├── API-v1-Reference.md
│   └── ...
│
└── index.md  (Documentation home)
```

3.4 Cross-References

Link to other documents using relative paths:

```markdown
<!-- Good: Relative links -->
See the [Naming Standard](../standards/CYBERCUBE-Naming-Identifier-Standard-v1.1.md)
for identifier conventions.

This decision is recorded in [ADR-0015](../adrs/ADR-0015-PostgreSQL-Selection.md).

<!-- Bad: Absolute paths or broken references -->
See /docs/standards/naming.md  <!-- Might break -->
See the Naming Standard.        <!-- No link -->
```

3.5 Tags and Metadata

Documents SHOULD use YAML frontmatter for structured metadata when tooling
supports it. Inline metadata (Status/Effective/Owner fields) is an acceptable
alternative:

```yaml
---
title: CYBERCUBE API Design Standard
type: standard
version: v1
status: active
effective_date: 2026-01-17
author: Platform Team
reviewers:
  - Alice Engineer
  - Bob Architect
tags:
  - api
  - design
  - rest
applies_to:
  - api-service
  - gateway
related:
  - CYBERCUBE-Naming-Identifier-Standard-v1.1
  - RFC-0023-API-Versioning
---
```

4. RFC Process

RFCs propose significant changes requiring broad input.

4.1 When to Write an RFC

**RFC required for:**
- New services or major components
- Changes affecting multiple teams
- Breaking API changes
- Security model changes
- New technology adoption
- Significant process changes

**RFC not required for:**
- Bug fixes
- Minor features
- Refactoring
- Documentation updates
- Configuration changes

4.2 RFC Lifecycle

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        RFC LIFECYCLE                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────────┐     │
│  │  DRAFT  │───▶│ REVIEW  │───▶│ DECISION│───▶│   FINAL     │     │
│  └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────────┘     │
│       │              │              │                │              │
│       │              │              ▼                ▼              │
│       │              │         Accept or        Accepted or        │
│       │              │         Reject or        Rejected           │
│       │              │         Defer                               │
│       │              │              │                               │
│       │              │              ├──▶ ┌──────────┐              │
│       │              │              │    │ DEFERRED │              │
│       │              │              │    └──────────┘              │
│       │              │              │    Revisit later              │
│       │              │              │                               │
│       │              │              └──▶ ┌────────────┐            │
│       │              │                   │ SUPERSEDED │            │
│       │              │                   └────────────┘            │
│       │              │                   Replaced by newer RFC      │
│       │              │                                              │
│       ▼              ▼                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────┐                                          │
│  │      WITHDRAWN       │  Author withdraws from Draft or Review   │
│  └──────────────────────┘                                          │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

4.3 RFC Status Values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| Draft | Under development by author |
| Review | Open for feedback |
| Accepted | Approved for implementation |
| Rejected | Not approved |
| Deferred | Postponed for future consideration |
| Superseded | Replaced by newer RFC |
| Withdrawn | Withdrawn by author |

4.4 RFC Process Steps

```markdown
## RFC Process

### 1. Draft (Author)
- [ ] Assign RFC number (sequential)
- [ ] Write proposal using RFC template
- [ ] Identify stakeholders and reviewers
- [ ] Create PR with RFC document

### 2. Review (2 weeks minimum)
- [ ] Announce RFC to stakeholders
- [ ] Collect feedback via PR comments
- [ ] Address questions and concerns
- [ ] Revise proposal as needed
- [ ] Mark as "Review" status

### 3. Decision Meeting
- [ ] Schedule decision meeting
- [ ] Invite key stakeholders
- [ ] Present final proposal
- [ ] Discuss outstanding concerns
- [ ] Make accept/reject decision

### 4. Finalize
- [ ] Update status (Accepted/Rejected)
- [ ] Document decision rationale
- [ ] Merge PR
- [ ] Announce decision
- [ ] Create implementation tasks (if accepted)
```

4.5 RFC Review Guidelines

**For reviewers:**
- Focus on the problem, not just the solution
- Suggest alternatives constructively
- Identify risks and edge cases
- Consider operational impact
- Evaluate against existing standards
- Provide actionable feedback

**Review questions:**
- Does this solve the stated problem?
- Are there simpler alternatives?
- What are the operational implications?
- How does this affect existing systems?
- What are the security implications?
- Is the implementation plan realistic?

4.6 RFC Registry

Maintain an index of all RFCs:

```markdown
# RFC Registry

## Active RFCs

| Number | Title | Status | Author | Created |
|--------|-------|--------|--------|---------|
| RFC-0045 | New Authentication System | Review | Alice | 2026-01-15 |
| RFC-0044 | GraphQL API Layer | Draft | Bob | 2026-01-10 |

## Accepted RFCs

| Number | Title | Accepted | Implementation |
|--------|-------|----------|----------------|
| RFC-0043 | Event Sourcing | 2026-01-05 | In Progress |
| RFC-0042 | Service Mesh | 2025-12-20 | Complete |

## Rejected/Withdrawn

| Number | Title | Outcome | Reason |
|--------|-------|---------|--------|
| RFC-0041 | NoSQL Migration | Rejected | Cost/benefit |
| RFC-0040 | Monorepo | Withdrawn | Scope change |
```

5. ADR Process

ADRs capture the context and consequences of architectural decisions.

5.1 When to Write an ADR

**ADR required for:**
- Technology selection (database, framework)
- Architectural patterns adopted
- Significant trade-offs made
- Decisions with long-term impact
- Reversing previous decisions

**ADR not required for:**
- Obvious choices (following standards)
- Temporary solutions
- Minor implementation details

5.2 ADR Lifecycle

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        ADR LIFECYCLE                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌────────────┐                   │
│  │ PROPOSED │───▶│ ACCEPTED │───▶│ DEPRECATED │                   │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └────────────┘                   │
│                        │                │                          │
│                        │                ▼                          │
│                        │         ┌────────────┐                    │
│                        │         │ SUPERSEDED │                    │
│                        │         └────────────┘                    │
│                        │                │                          │
│                        │                ▼                          │
│                        │         New ADR replaces                  │
│                        │                                           │
│                        └────────────────────────────────────────▶  │
│                              Still valid, in use                   │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

5.3 ADR Writing Tips

**Context section:**
- Describe the situation objectively
- Include constraints and requirements
- Note relevant background

**Decision section:**
- State the decision clearly
- Be specific and actionable
- Include what we will NOT do

**Consequences section:**
- List positive outcomes
- Acknowledge negative trade-offs
- Note neutral observations

```markdown
## Context

We need a primary database for user data. Requirements:
- ACID transactions
- Strong consistency
- Horizontal scaling potential
- Team familiarity
- Managed service available

Current options: PostgreSQL, MySQL, CockroachDB

## Decision

We will use **PostgreSQL** as our primary relational database.

We will NOT use:
- MySQL: Less robust JSON support
- CockroachDB: Overkill for current scale, higher cost

## Consequences

### Positive
- Team has deep PostgreSQL expertise
- Excellent JSON/JSONB support for flexible schemas
- Strong ecosystem (pgvector, PostGIS)
- Cloud Run/Cloud SQL integration

### Negative
- Single-region limitation initially
- Manual sharding if needed later
- No built-in multi-master

### Neutral
- Standard SQL, portable queries
- Well-documented operational procedures
```

5.4 ADR Registry

```markdown
# ADR Registry

## Current Decisions

| Number | Title | Date | Status |
|--------|-------|------|--------|
| ADR-0020 | Use OpenTelemetry | 2026-01-10 | Accepted |
| ADR-0019 | Event-Driven Architecture | 2026-01-05 | Accepted |
| ADR-0018 | TypeScript Everywhere | 2025-12-15 | Accepted |

## Superseded Decisions

| Number | Title | Superseded By | Date |
|--------|-------|---------------|------|
| ADR-0005 | Use REST API | ADR-0019 | 2025-06-01 |
| ADR-0003 | Use MongoDB | ADR-0015 | 2025-03-01 |
```

6. Versioning

Documents are versioned to track changes and ensure clarity.

6.1 Version Format

**Standards and Policies:**
```
v{major}.{minor}

v1.0  - Initial release
v1.1  - Minor additions/clarifications
v2.0  - Breaking changes
```

**RFCs and ADRs:**
```
No version numbers
Document status changes instead
Superseded documents link to replacements
```

**Guides and Runbooks:**
```
Date-based: Last Updated: YYYY-MM-DD
Or version: v{major}.{minor}
```

6.2 Version Incrementing

| Change Type | Version Increment | Example |
|-------------|-------------------|---------|
| Typo fixes | None | v1.0 → v1.0 |
| Clarifications | Minor | v1.0 → v1.1 |
| New sections | Minor | v1.1 → v1.2 |
| Breaking changes | Major | v1.2 → v2.0 |
| Restructuring | Major | v1.2 → v2.0 |
| Removing requirements | Major | v1.2 → v2.0 |

6.3 Version History

Every versioned document includes version history:

```markdown
## Version History

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| v2.0 | 2026-01-17 | Alice | Major restructure, new auth requirements |
| v1.2 | 2025-11-01 | Bob | Added API versioning section |
| v1.1 | 2025-08-15 | Alice | Clarified naming conventions |
| v1.0 | 2025-06-01 | Alice | Initial release |
```

6.4 Breaking Changes

Breaking changes to standards require:

1. **Announcement:** Notify affected teams 30+ days ahead
2. **Migration guide:** Document how to comply
3. **Grace period:** Allow time for migration
4. **Support:** Help teams transition

```markdown
## Breaking Changes in v2.0

### BC-1: New identifier format

**Previous:** `usr_abc123`
**New:** `usr_A1B2C3D4E5F6`

**Migration:**
1. Update ID generation to new format
2. Both formats accepted during transition (until 2026-06-01)
3. Run migration script for existing IDs

**Timeline:**
- 2026-01-17: v2.0 released
- 2026-03-17: New format required for new records
- 2026-06-01: Old format deprecated
```

6.5 Document Deprecation

Process for deprecating documents:

```markdown
## Deprecation Notice

> ⚠️ **DEPRECATED:** This document is deprecated as of 2026-01-17.
>
> **Reason:** Superseded by [New Standard](./New-Standard-v1.md)
>
> **Migration:** See [Migration Guide](./migration.md)
>
> **Removal Date:** 2026-07-17

---

{Original content below, unchanged}
```

7. Ownership & Governance

Clear ownership ensures documents remain accurate and current.

7.1 Ownership Model

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      OWNERSHIP MODEL                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  OWNER                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Accountable for document accuracy and currency               │  │
│  │ Can delegate but not abdicate                                │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                              │                                      │
│              ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐                     │
│              ▼               ▼               ▼                     │
│       ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐                │
│       │ AUTHOR   │    │MAINTAINER│    │APPROVER  │                │
│       │          │    │          │    │          │                │
│       │ Creates  │    │ Updates  │    │ Reviews  │                │
│       │ content  │    │ content  │    │ changes  │                │
│       └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘                │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

7.2 Roles and Responsibilities

| Role | Responsibility | Typical Assignment |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| Owner | Accountable for accuracy | Team lead, manager |
| Author | Creates initial content | Individual contributor |
| Maintainer | Keeps content current | Team member |
| Approver | Reviews and approves | Tech lead, architect |
| Contributor | Provides input | Anyone |

7.3 Ownership by Document Type

| Type | Owner | Approver |
|------|-------|----------|
| Standard | Responsible team lead | Architecture team |
| Policy | Relevant director | Leadership |
| RFC | Author | Stakeholders |
| ADR | Decision maker | Architecture team |
| Guide | Team lead | Team |
| Runbook | On-call team lead | Team |
| Reference | API/Service owner | Team |

7.4 Ownership Transfer

When ownership changes:

```markdown
# Ownership Transfer: {Document Name}

**Date:** 2026-01-17
**From:** Alice (alice@cybercube.software)
**To:** Bob (bob@cybercube.software)

## Transfer Checklist
- [ ] Review document for accuracy
- [ ] Update owner in metadata
- [ ] Transfer any related tasks
- [ ] Notify stakeholders
- [ ] Update team documentation

## Handoff Notes
{Any context the new owner needs}
```

7.5 Review Cycles

| Document Type | Review Frequency | Trigger |
|---------------|------------------|---------|
| Standard | Annually | Major system changes |
| Policy | Annually | Compliance requirements |
| RFC | N/A | One-time decision |
| ADR | N/A | When superseded |
| Guide | Quarterly | Process changes |
| Runbook | Quarterly | Incident learnings |
| Reference | On API change | Release cycle |

7.6 Stale Document Handling

Identify and address stale documents:

```markdown
## Stale Document Policy

### Identification
Documents are flagged stale if:
- Not reviewed in 2x review cycle
- Referenced system no longer exists
- Author/owner has left organization
- Multiple reported inaccuracies

### Resolution
1. Assign new owner (if needed)
2. Review and update content
3. Mark deprecated (if obsolete)
4. Archive (if no longer relevant)

### Archive Policy
Archived documents:
- Moved to `docs/archive/`
- Preserved for reference
- Excluded from search
- Marked with archive date
```

8. Style Guide

Consistent style improves readability and professionalism.

8.1 Language and Tone

**Use:**
- Active voice
- Present tense
- Second person ("you") for instructions
- Direct, concise sentences
- Technical terms consistently

**Avoid:**
- Passive voice (unless necessary)
- Future tense (use present)
- Jargon without definition
- Unnecessarily long sentences
- Ambiguous pronouns

```markdown
<!-- Good -->
To create a user, call the POST /users endpoint.
The server returns a 201 status code on success.

<!-- Bad -->
A user can be created by calling the POST /users endpoint.
A 201 status code will be returned by the server when the operation has been completed successfully.
```

8.2 Formatting Conventions

**Headings:**
```markdown
# Document Title (H1) — One per document
## Major Section (H2)
### Subsection (H3)
#### Detail (H4) — Use sparingly
```

**Lists:**
```markdown
<!-- Use bullets for unordered items -->
- Item one
- Item two

<!-- Use numbers for sequences -->
1. First step
2. Second step

<!-- Use tables for comparisons -->
| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
```

**Code:**
```markdown
<!-- Inline code for short references -->
Use the `createUser()` function.

<!-- Code blocks for examples -->
```typescript
function createUser(email: string): User {
  // implementation
}
```
```

8.3 Terminology Consistency

Use consistent terms throughout:

| Use | Don't Use |
|-----|-----------|
| user | customer, client (unless specific) |
| API | api, Api |
| ID | Id, id (in prose; `id` is acceptable in code/schema contexts) |
| database | DB, db (in prose) |
| CYBERCUBE | Cybercube, CyberCube |

8.4 Document Metadata

Every document includes:

```markdown
**Status:** {Draft|Active|Deprecated}
**Effective:** {YYYY-MM-DD} (for standards)
**Last Updated:** {YYYY-MM-DD}
**Owner:** {Name or Team}
**Version:** {vX.Y} (for versioned docs)
```

8.5 Diagrams and Visuals

**ASCII diagrams** for inline documentation:
```
┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐
│ Client  │────▶│   API   │
└─────────┘     └─────────┘
```

**Image files** for complex diagrams:
- Store in `docs/images/`
- Name: `{document-name}-{diagram-name}.png`
- Include alt text
- Prefer SVG for scalability

```markdown
![Authentication flow](./images/auth-standard-flow.svg)
```

8.6 Links and References

**Internal links:** Relative paths
```markdown
See [Naming Standard](../standards/CYBERCUBE-Naming-Standard-v1.md)
```

**External links:** Full URLs with context
```markdown
Based on [RFC 2119](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2119.txt) keywords.
```

**Section links:** Use anchors
```markdown
See [Error Handling](#error-handling) below.
```

9. Tooling & Workflow

Standard tools and workflows support documentation.

9.1 Documentation Tools

| Purpose | Tool | Notes |
|---------|------|-------|
| Authoring | Markdown | CommonMark + GFM |
| Version control | Git | Same repo as code |
| Review | Pull requests | Same process as code |
| Rendering | GitHub/Notion | Auto-renders Markdown |
| Search | GitHub search | Native search |
| Diagrams | Mermaid/ASCII | Inline diagrams |

9.2 Documentation Workflow

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  DOCUMENTATION WORKFLOW                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  1. CREATE           2. REVIEW           3. PUBLISH                │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐         │
│  │ Write in     │───▶│ Create PR    │───▶│ Merge to     │         │
│  │ Markdown     │    │ Get reviews  │    │ main branch  │         │
│  │ Branch       │    │ Address      │    │ Auto-publish │         │
│  └──────────────┘    │ feedback     │    └──────────────┘         │
│                      └──────────────┘                              │
│                                                                     │
│  4. MAINTAIN                                                       │
│  ┌──────────────┐                                                  │
│  │ Review cycle │                                                  │
│  │ Update as    │                                                  │
│  │ needed       │                                                  │
│  └──────────────┘                                                  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

9.3 Pull Request Template for Docs

```markdown
## Documentation Change

### Type
- [ ] New document
- [ ] Update existing
- [ ] Deprecation
- [ ] Fix/correction

### Document
- **Path:** docs/...
- **Type:** Standard / RFC / ADR / Guide / Runbook / Reference

### Summary
{What changed and why}

### Checklist
- [ ] Follows style guide
- [ ] Links verified
- [ ] Spell-checked
- [ ] Reviewed by owner (if not author)
- [ ] Metadata updated
- [ ] Version incremented (if applicable)
```

9.4 Automated Checks

```yaml
# .github/workflows/docs.yml
name: Documentation

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'docs/**'

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Lint Markdown
        uses: DavidAnson/markdownlint-cli2-action@v14
        with:
          globs: 'docs/**/*.md'
      
      - name: Check links
        uses: lycheeverse/lychee-action@v1
        with:
          args: --verbose docs/**/*.md
      
      - name: Spell check
        uses: streetsidesoftware/cspell-action@v5
        with:
          files: docs/**/*.md
```

9.5 Documentation as Code

Documentation lives alongside code:

```
project/
├── src/               # Source code
├── tests/             # Tests
├── docs/              # Documentation
│   ├── standards/
│   ├── guides/
│   ├── rfcs/
│   ├── adrs/
│   └── runbooks/
├── README.md          # Project overview
└── CONTRIBUTING.md    # How to contribute
```

Benefits:
- Same review process
- Same version control
- Changes tracked with code
- Stays in sync with implementation

CYBERCUBE Documentation — Quick Reference Card

Print it. Keep it handy.

🔹 Document Types

| Type | Compliance | Use For |
|------|------------|---------|
| Standard | MUST | Requirements |
| Policy | MUST | Positions |
| RFC | N/A | Proposals |
| ADR | Info | Decisions |
| Guide | SHOULD | Recommendations |
| Runbook | Required | Procedures |
| Reference | Info | Specifications |

🔹 RFC 2119 Keywords

| Keyword | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| MUST | Required |
| SHOULD | Recommended |
| MAY | Optional |

🔹 Document Naming

```
Standard: CYBERCUBE-{Name}-Standard-v{X}.md
RFC:      RFC-{number}-{Name}.md
ADR:      ADR-{number}-{Name}.md
Guide:    {Name}-Guide.md
Runbook:  Runbook-{Name}.md
```

🔹 Version Format

```
Standards: v{major}.{minor}
Minor: Additions/clarifications
Major: Breaking changes
```

🔹 RFC Process

```
DRAFT → REVIEW (2 weeks) → DECISION → FINAL
```

🔹 ADR Structure

```
Context → Decision → Consequences
```

🔹 Review Cycles

| Type | Frequency |
|------|-----------|
| Standard | Annual |
| Guide | Quarterly |
| Runbook | Quarterly |

🔹 Ownership Roles

| Role | Does |
|------|------|
| Owner | Accountable |
| Author | Creates |
| Maintainer | Updates |
| Approver | Reviews |

🔹 Style Rules

✅ Active voice
✅ Present tense
✅ Direct sentences
✅ Consistent terms
❌ Passive voice
❌ Undefined jargon

🔹 Required Metadata

```
Status: {Draft|Active|Deprecated}
Effective: {YYYY-MM-DD}
Owner: {Name/Team}
Version: {vX.Y}
```

🔹 Workflow

```
Write → PR → Review → Merge → Maintain
```

🔹 Don't Forget

✅ One owner per document
✅ Version history maintained
✅ Links are relative
✅ Review cycles scheduled
✅ Deprecate, don't delete
✅ Docs live with code

---

## Appendix X: Shared Roles (Named Approvers)

Many standards reference the same small set of approvers under different names (CAB member, change approver, security reviewer, incident commander, audit sponsor). To avoid divergence, the canonical role bindings below are the default referenced across the portfolio. A standard that needs a different binding MUST state so explicitly.

### X.1 Canonical Approver Trio

| Role Key | Default Holder | Backup | Used by |
|----------|----------------|--------|---------|
| `eng-lead` | Engineering Lead | Deputy Engineering Lead | Change approval (POL-ENG-001), architectural decisions, RFC acceptance |
| `sec-lead` | Security Lead | Deputy Security Lead | Security review, incident commander (SEV1/SEV2), exceptions gate, vulnerability triage |
| `oncall-sre` | Current on-call SRE (rotation) | Secondary on-call | Production change sign-off, incident response paging target, runbook ownership |

### X.2 Binding rules

1. Every CYBERCUBE project MUST publish a `roles.md` (or equivalent) that binds these three keys to named people for that project.
2. Changes to the binding MUST be logged (who, when, why) and communicated at least 5 business days before effect, except in emergency (documented post-hoc within 2 business days).
3. If a standard references a role not in the canonical set (e.g. `records-custodian`, `dpo`, `records-manager`), it MUST either (a) define the new role locally with scope and escalation path, or (b) map it to one of the canonical three.
4. The trio is **load-bearing** for change, incident, and security decisions. Projects that cannot name all three (e.g. solo projects) MUST escalate at least one role to a CYBERCUBE-wide default holder.

### X.3 Standards that reference this appendix

- `POL-ENG-001` Change Management (CAB composition)
- `STD-OPS-004` Incident Response (incident commander, on-call target)
- `STD-GOV-004` Internal Audit & Assurance (audit sponsor = `eng-lead`)
- `POL-AI-001` AI Usage & Ethics (T3 approval gate)
- `POL-GOV-001` Standards Governance (exception approvers per §8.3)

> Adding a standard to this list requires only referencing Appendix X by tag `[roles:X.1]`; no local re-definition needed.
